name: Edge-Case Safety Expansion Pack
description: High-risk mixed-signal records for chest symptoms, low glucose, medicine reactions, child fever, stroke-like headache, palpitations, dehydration, and repeat blood-pressure checks.
reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and safety review before production medical use

records.title: Chest Pressure, Sweating, and Breathing Warning Review
records.summary: Separates urgent chest-pressure patterns from routine discomfort by focusing on sweating, breathlessness, exertion, jaw or arm radiation, collapse, and the need for immediate real-world assessment.
records.safetyNotes: Active chest pressure with sweating, breathing trouble, fainting, jaw or arm pain, or fast worsening symptoms needs urgent medical care now.
whatToTrack: Whether the pressure started at rest or with activity and whether it is spreading to the jaw, arm, or back.
Sweating, breathlessness, nausea, faintness, collapse, and any prior heart or blood-pressure history.
Time of onset, repeat episodes, and whether symptoms are worsening over minutes.
careQuestions: Which features make this a time-critical chest warning rather than routine discomfort?
What history, medicines, and symptom timing should be ready for urgent review?
precautions: Do not stay in routine monitoring mode for active chest pressure with sweating or breathing trouble.
Do not drive yourself if symptoms feel severe, unstable, or rapidly worsening.

records.title: Low Sugar, Shaking, Sweating, and Confusion Review
records.summary: Organizes possible low-sugar episodes around shakiness, sweating, confusion, faintness, food timing, insulin or diabetes medicines, and urgent escalation when the person cannot recover safely.
records.safetyNotes: Confusion, seizure, unconsciousness, repeated vomiting, or inability to take sugar safely needs urgent real-world help.
whatToTrack: Glucose reading if available, last meal, last diabetes medicine timing, and whether symptoms improved after sugar intake.
Shaking, sweating, confusion, faintness, seizure, or trouble swallowing safely.
Recent illness, vomiting, heavy activity, alcohol use, and whether another person is present.
careQuestions: Which symptoms suggest a routine low-sugar recovery versus an urgent emergency response?
Which medicine, meal, or illness details matter most for the next diabetes follow-up?
precautions: Do not keep self-managing if the person is confused, fainting, or cannot safely swallow.
Do not guess future diabetes-dose changes from this app.

records.title: Medicine Rash, Swelling, and Breathing-Reaction Review
records.summary: Separates mild medicine-skin questions from urgent allergy patterns by focusing on swelling, hives, breathing change, throat tightness, fever, blistering, and timing after a new medicine.
records.safetyNotes: Breathing trouble, face or throat swelling, faintness, or rapidly worsening rash needs urgent care now.
whatToTrack: The medicine name, first dose time, rash start time, spread pattern, and whether any other new product was used.
Any hives, lip or tongue swelling, throat tightness, wheeze, dizziness, or faintness.
Fever, blistering, mouth sores, or skin pain that could change urgency.
careQuestions: Which rash patterns suggest mild follow-up and which need urgent allergy review now?
What medicine-label, allergy-history, and photo details would help the next clinician or pharmacist review?
precautions: Do not treat breathing trouble or facial swelling like a simple side effect.
Do not restart a suspect medicine without pharmacist or clinician direction.

records.title: Child Fever, Poor Drinking, and Breathing Review
records.summary: Builds a pediatric safety review around fever, drinking, urine output, breathing effort, lethargy, rash, vomiting, and caregiver concern so higher-risk child patterns surface early.
records.safetyNotes: Breathing trouble, blue lips, dehydration, unusual sleepiness, seizure, stiff neck, or a child who cannot drink should be reviewed urgently.
whatToTrack: Temperature pattern, age, drinking, urine output, tears, activity level, and whether the child is waking normally.
Fast breathing, chest pulling, blue color, rash, vomiting, seizure, or stiff neck.
When symptoms started, whether they are worsening, and any sick contacts or new medicines.
careQuestions: Which child-fever features move this from home observation to same-day or urgent evaluation?
What hydration and breathing details matter most for pediatric follow-up?
precautions: Do not rely on routine monitoring if the child is hard to wake, breathing poorly, or not drinking.
Use urgent review sooner when caregiver concern is rising quickly or the pattern feels unusual.

records.title: Sudden Severe Headache and Stroke-Warning Review
records.summary: Keeps severe sudden headache on a neurologic safety path by combining thunderclap onset, weakness, speech or vision change, confusion, neck stiffness, and blood-pressure context.
records.safetyNotes: A sudden severe headache with weakness, speech trouble, confusion, seizure, fainting, or vision change needs urgent assessment now.
whatToTrack: Exact onset, whether it peaked suddenly, and any trigger such as exertion, injury, or blood-pressure spike.
Weakness, numbness, speech trouble, confusion, vision change, seizure, or fainting.
Neck stiffness, vomiting, fever, and whether this feels different from prior headaches.
careQuestions: Which severe-headache features point to urgent neurologic review instead of routine migraine-style follow-up?
What timing and symptom details matter most for emergency or same-day review?
precautions: Do not label a sudden severe headache as routine if new neurologic symptoms are present.
Do not keep waiting for home readings if the headache is escalating or the person seems altered.

records.title: Palpitations, Caffeine, and Dizziness Review
records.summary: Organizes racing-heart and palpitation questions around caffeine, dehydration, anxiety, illness, pulse pattern, chest symptoms, and fainting risk while keeping urgent cardiac warning signs visible.
records.safetyNotes: Palpitations with fainting, chest pain, severe breathlessness, or collapse need prompt medical review.
whatToTrack: Whether the rhythm feels fast, irregular, or skipped and whether it happens at rest, after caffeine, with illness, or during stress.
Pulse or heart-rate readings if available, along with chest discomfort, breathlessness, fainting, or severe dizziness.
Hydration, alcohol, stimulant use, poor sleep, and any recent medicine changes.
careQuestions: Which palpitation patterns suggest routine follow-up and which need same-day or urgent review?
What trigger, pulse, and symptom details would make the next cardiology discussion more precise?
precautions: Do not dismiss palpitations that come with fainting, chest symptoms, or severe breathlessness.
Avoid stacking caffeine or stimulant triggers when the pattern is still unclear.

records.title: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Dehydration, and Kidney-Medicine Review
records.summary: Connects vomiting or diarrhea with dehydration, urine output, dizziness, blood-pressure or diabetes medicines, and the need for pharmacist or clinician review when the person cannot keep up with fluids.
records.safetyNotes: Repeated vomiting, confusion, fainting, reduced urine, severe weakness, or worsening illness needs prompt medical review.
whatToTrack: Vomiting or diarrhea frequency, ability to drink, urine output, dizziness, and any blood-pressure or glucose readings.
Which medicines were taken recently, especially kidney-, blood-pressure-, or diabetes-related medicines.
Weakness, confusion, fainting, fever, and whether the illness is improving or worsening.
careQuestions: Which dehydration signs mean medicine questions should move from routine review to prompt clinician or pharmacist review?
What urine, fluid, and medicine-timing details matter most for the next follow-up?
precautions: Do not keep relying on home monitoring if fluid loss is continuing and urine output is dropping.
Do not guess which chronic medicines to stop or restart without pharmacist or clinician review.

records.title: Repeat Blood Pressure, Technique, and Headache Review
records.summary: Focuses on repeat blood-pressure checks by combining seated rest, cuff technique, headache or dizziness symptoms, baseline comparison, and when a high reading should move beyond home monitoring.
records.safetyNotes: Very high repeated readings with neurologic, chest, breathing, or fainting symptoms need urgent real-world care.
whatToTrack: Two repeat readings after seated rest, cuff position, cuff size, arm level, and whether the device is working normally.
Headache, dizziness, vision change, weakness, chest symptoms, breathing trouble, and comparison with usual baseline.
Recent caffeine, pain, stress, missed medicines, activity, and whether readings stay elevated.
careQuestions: Which repeat-reading pattern is reasonable to track and which pattern needs same-day or urgent review?
What technique issues or symptom combinations should change how the next BP result is interpreted?
precautions: Do not rely on one rushed home reading when symptoms are active and technique may be off.
Do not ignore repeat high readings when headache, dizziness, weakness, chest discomfort, or breathlessness are present.
